title: Plan for Change
description: Create custom change objects and user-defined groups. Configure change management preferences, task form templates, association rules, mapping rules, and change intent.

topics.title: Change Objects and User-Defined Groups

content.text: Before tailoring a change management process, you need custom change objects and organized user groups. Windchill's Type and Attribute Management utility lets you create subtypes of existing change objects, while user-defined groups organize users for targeted task routing within business processes.
Creating Custom Change Object Subtypes

Custom change objects are created as subtypes of existing Windchill types. A subtype inherits all properties and behaviors from its parent type, then adds or overrides attributes to meet your specific process needs. For the Delta Drills scenario, two subtypes are required: Delta Review (a subtype of Review Object) for regulation reviews, and Delta New Regulation Document (a subtype of WTDocument) for tracking regulation documents.

rows: Tracks regulation review activities in the Delta Drills change process
Stores regulation documents that trigger change requests
Defines the implementation task assigned to change implementers
Defines the regulatory review task assigned to regulation reviewers

content.text: User-Defined Groups

User-defined groups organize users by business function. Unlike Windchill's built-in context teams, user-defined groups can be assigned to roles in team templates for targeted task routing. For Delta Drills, two groups are needed: the Delta Regulation Group (regulation reviewers who evaluate new regulations) and the Delta Change Group (change implementers who execute change tasks).

User-defined groups provide three key benefits: they organize users by business function, they can be assigned to workflow activities for targeted task routing, and they enable granular access control for change objects.

content.prompt: Match each group to its purpose in the Delta Drills process:

pairs.right: Reviews new regulations and evaluates compliance impact
Implements change tasks and executes engineering changes
Tracks regulation review activities as a Review Object subtype
Stores regulation documents as a WTDocument subtype

keyTakeaways: Custom change objects are created as subtypes that inherit all properties from their parent type.
User-defined groups organize users by business function and can be assigned to roles in team templates.
The Delta Drills scenario requires two subtypes (Delta Review, Delta New Regulation Document) and two groups (Delta Regulation Group, Delta Change Group).
Subtypes are created using the Type and Attribute Management utility under Site > Utilities.

topics.title: Change Preferences and Task Form Templates

content.text: Windchill provides a set of Change Management Preferences that control how change objects behave. These preferences are configured at the organization or product level and determine whether features like sequenced plans, task templates, and auto-association are available in your change process.
Six Key Change Management Preferences

cards.back: Allows creating a Change Notice directly without first creating a Change Request. Useful when the change is pre-approved or originates from a regulation rather than a formal request.

Enables ordering change tasks in a specific sequence within a Change Notice. Tasks execute in the defined order rather than all at once, which is critical for processes where one task must complete before the next begins.

Enables the use of predefined task templates when a Change Notice is submitted. Templates automatically create tasks with default names, assignees, reviewers, and sequencing - reducing manual setup and ensuring consistency.
Automatically associates change objects when they are created in the context of another change object. For example, creating a Change Notice from a Change Request automatically links them.

rows: Yes - regulation changes skip the CR step
Yes - regulatory review must precede implementation
Yes - auto-creates Delta Change Task and Delta Regulatory Task
No - assignees and reviewers are required
No - tasks are created via templates, not embedded

content.text: Task Form Templates

Task Form Templates define the default tasks that are automatically created when a Change Notice is submitted. Each template specifies the task name, default assignee role, reviewer, and sequence position. For Delta Drills, two templates are needed: Delta Change Task (assigned to the Delta Change Group for implementation) and Delta Regulatory Task (assigned to the Delta Regulation Group for compliance review).

content.prompt: Match each Change Management preference to what it enables:

pairs.right: Create a Change Notice directly, skipping the Change Request step
Execute change tasks in a specific order rather than all at once
Auto-generate tasks with predefined names, assignees, and sequencing
Automatically link change objects created in the context of another

content.text: Task Form Templates save significant time by eliminating manual task creation. When a Change Notice is submitted, the templates automatically generate all required tasks with the correct assignees and sequencing. Always verify that the Task Form Templates preference is enabled before creating templates.

keyTakeaways: Six Change Management Preferences control the behavior of change objects: CN without CR, Sequenced Plan, Task Form Templates, Auto Associate, Optional Assignee/Reviewer, and Embedded Change Task.
Task Form Templates automatically create tasks with predefined names, assignees, and sequencing when a Change Notice is submitted.
The Task Form Templates preference must be enabled before templates can be created or used.
Delta Drills requires CN without CR, Sequenced Plan, and Task Form Templates all set to Yes.

topics.title: Association and Mapping Rules

content.text: Windchill uses three categories of business rules to control how change objects interact: Business Rules, Association Rules, and Mapping Rules. Association Rules are particularly important because they define how change objects link to each other, controlling the relationships that drive your change process.
Association Rules

An Association Rule defines how two change object types link to each other. Each rule specifies five properties: the association type, the two object types being linked (Role A and Role B), the cardinality of the relationship, the owning role (which object's modify access is needed), and an optional required role constraint.

rows: Links objects within the same change process flow
Links a problem report or request to the change that addresses it
Change Request addressed by Change Notice
Links change objects that are related but not in a direct process flow
Two Change Notices affecting the same product
Links additional affected objects to a change report
Additional parts reported against a Problem Report

content.text: For the Delta Drills scenario, the association rule uses Change Process type, linking Delta Review (Role A) to Change Notice (Role B) with 1:many cardinality. The owning role is Role B, meaning you need modify access to the Change Notice to create the link.
Association Rule Properties

content.prompt: Match each association rule property to its definition:

pairs.right: Defines the category of relationship (Change Process, Addressed By, etc.)
Defines how many objects can participate (1:1, 1:many, many:many)
Determines which object's modify access is required to create the link
Constraint specifying which role must exist for the association to be created

keyTakeaways: Three categories of business rules control change objects: Business Rules, Association Rules, and Mapping Rules.
Association Rules define how change object types link to each other using five properties: association type, Role A, Role B, cardinality, and owning role.
Four association types are available: Change Process, Addressed By, Related Change, and Additional Reported Against.
The owning role determines which object's modify access is needed to create the link between objects.

topics.title: Change Intent and Planning

content.text: Mapping Rules extend the change management configuration by connecting change intents, release targets, and object states. Three types of mapping rules work together to define how affected objects are planned, tracked, and released through the change process.
Three Types of Mapping Rules

cards.back: Maps a change intent value to a release target for affected object types. For example, mapping the intent 'Change' to the release target 'Production' for Delta New Regulation Document.
Maps an affected object type to the available change intent values. Defines which intents (such as Change, Add, Remove) are valid for each object type.
Maps an affected object type to its released state. This rule controls whether redline markup can be created for parts and documents. Without this rule, redlines cannot be created.

content.text: Change Intent as a Planning Tool

Change Intent is an optional field on each affected object in a Change Notice. It communicates the intended product change separately from the actual revision. This separation allows planners to indicate what needs to happen (add a part, change a document, remove a component) before execution begins. To see Change Intent in the Affected Objects and Resulting Objects tables, you must create a custom view - it is not visible in the default table layout.

The Object Release States mapping rule is required for redline creation. Without this rule configured for a given object type, Windchill cannot determine the released state and will not allow redline markup to be created for parts or documents.

content.prompt: Arrange the mapping rule configuration steps in the correct order:
items: Create Object Change Intents rule to define valid intent values for each object type
Create Change Intent Release Targets rule to map intents to release targets
Create Object Release States rule to map object types to released states
Create a custom view to display Change Intent in Affected/Resulting Objects tables

keyTakeaways: Three mapping rule types work together: Change Intent Release Targets, Object Change Intents, and Object Release States.
Change Intent provides planning separate from revision - it communicates what needs to happen before execution begins.
The Object Release States mapping rule is required for redline creation on parts and documents.
A custom view must be created to display Change Intent in the Affected Objects and Resulting Objects tables.

topics.title: Create Change Objects and Groups

content.text: In this exercise, you create custom change object subtypes and user-defined groups for the Delta Drills regulation review process. You will use the Type and Attribute Management utility to create subtypes and the Organization Members page to create groups.
This exercise combines creating both custom subtypes and user-defined groups. Ensure you have administrator access to the Site > Utilities area and the PTC Power Equipment organization.

content.title: Create Change Objects and Groups
content.objective: Create custom change object subtypes and user-defined groups for the Delta Drills regulation review process.

steps.action: If necessary, open a browser and click the Windchill bookmark.
steps.detail: Launches the Windchill web client in your browser.

steps.action: From the Select Your User page, select Mike Jones.
steps.detail: Mike Jones is the organization administrator who will configure the change process.

steps.action: Open the Navigator and select Organizations > PTC Power Equipment > Utilities.
steps.detail: Navigates to the organization-level Utilities page where administrative tools are located.

steps.action: Under the Business Administration heading, select Type and Attribute Management.
steps.detail: Type and Attribute Management is the central utility for creating and managing object types and their attributes.

steps.action: Open the Manage Types bar on the left of the page.
steps.detail: The Manage Types panel displays the type hierarchy tree used to navigate to specific object types.

steps.action: Scroll down to Review and expand the node.
steps.detail: The Review type is the parent type that contains Design Review and Peer Review subtypes.

steps.action: Right-click Review and select New Subtype.
steps.detail: Creates a new subtype that inherits all properties and behaviors from the Review parent type.

steps.action:
Enter the following for the listed fields:
Internal Name: DeltaReview (no space)
Display Name: Delta Review
Instantiable: Yes
steps.detail: The Internal Name is the system identifier (no spaces allowed). The Display Name is what users see in the UI.
steps.hint: Setting Instantiable to Yes lets you create new Delta Review objects.

steps.action: Click OK to create the subtype.
steps.detail: The Delta Review subtype is now available under the Review node in the type hierarchy.

steps.action: In the Manage Types tree, find the Document type.
steps.detail: The Document category contains WTDocument and its existing subtypes.

steps.action: Right-click Document and select New Subtype.
steps.detail: Creates a new document subtype that inherits all properties from the Document parent type.

steps.action:
Enter the following for the listed fields:
Internal Name: DeltaNewRegulationDoc (no space)
Display Name: Delta New Regulation Document
Instantiable: Yes
steps.detail: This subtype will store regulation documents that trigger change requests in the Delta Drills process.

steps.action: Click OK to create the subtype.
steps.detail: The Delta New Regulation Document subtype is now available under the Document node.

steps.action: Click Done.
steps.detail: Closes the type creation workflow.

steps.action: Close the Type - Delta New Regulation Document tab.
steps.detail: Returns you to the main Type and Attribute Management view.

steps.action: Open the Navigator and select Organizations > PTC Power Equipment > Groups.
steps.detail: The Groups page displays all user-defined groups within the PTC Power Equipment organization.
steps.hint: You should be signed in to Windchill as mjones/ptc.

steps.action: Click the Create new group icon.
steps.detail: Opens the New Group dialog for creating a user-defined group.

steps.action: In the New Group dialog, type Delta Regulation Group in the Name field.
steps.detail: This group will contain regulation reviewers who evaluate new regulations for compliance impact.

steps.action: In the Description field, type Complete change tasks necessary for compliance with new regulation.
steps.detail: A clear description helps administrators understand the group's purpose.

steps.action: Click Next.
steps.detail: Advances to the Members step where you add users to the group.

steps.action: At the top of the Members table, click the Add members to group icon.
steps.detail: Opens the Find Participant dialog for searching and selecting users.

steps.action: In the Find Participant window, click Search.
steps.detail: Displays all available participants in the search results.

steps.action: Press Ctrl and select: Anna Chen, Kaylee Fry, Mike Jones, and Simon Tam.
steps.detail: These four users will be members of the Delta Regulation Group, responsible for regulation review tasks.
steps.hint: Hold the Ctrl key while clicking each name to select multiple users.

steps.action: Click Add. Click OK to close the Find Participant dialog and return the results to the Members table.
steps.detail: The selected users are now listed as members of the Delta Regulation Group.

steps.action: In the New Group dialog, click Apply.
steps.detail: Creates the Delta Regulation Group and keeps the dialog open so you can create the second group.

steps.action: In the New Group dialog, type Delta Change Group in the Name field.
steps.detail: This group will contain change implementers who execute engineering change tasks.

steps.action: In the Description field, type Complete change tasks.
steps.detail: Describes the purpose of the Delta Change Group.

steps.action: Click Next.
steps.detail: Advances to the Members step for the second group.

steps.action: At the top of the Members table, click the Add members to group icon.
steps.detail: Opens the Find Participant dialog for the second group.

steps.action: In the Find Participant window, click Search.
steps.detail: Displays all available participants.

steps.action: Press Ctrl and select: Sharon Abrams, Hans Christensen, Jayne Cobb, and Mike Jones.
steps.detail: These four users will be members of the Delta Change Group.
steps.hint: Mike Jones is included in both groups to make it easier for testing your process later.

steps.action: Click Add. Click OK to close the Find Participant dialog and return the results to the Members table.
steps.detail: The selected users are now listed as members of the Delta Change Group.

steps.action: In the New Group dialog, click Finish.
steps.detail: Creates the Delta Change Group and closes the dialog. Both groups are now available in the Groups table.

steps.action: Open the Navigator and select Drill-1000 Series > Team.
steps.detail: Navigates to the team configuration for the Drill 1000 product context where roles are managed.

steps.action: From the Actions menu, select Add Roles.
steps.detail: Opens the dialog to add new roles to the product team.

steps.action: In the Add Roles to Team dialog, select Delta Regulation Manager from the list and click OK.
steps.detail: Adds the Delta Regulation Manager role to the Drill 1000 product team.

steps.action: Click the Add participants to team icon next to Delta Regulation Manager in the Members table.
steps.detail: Opens the participant search dialog for the Delta Regulation Manager role.

steps.action: Set the Search for field to Group.
steps.detail: Filters the search to show only user-defined groups instead of individual users.

steps.action: In the Group Name field, type Delta* and click Search.
steps.detail: The wildcard search finds both Delta groups you created.

steps.action: Select Delta Regulation Group from the Search Results list and click Add.
steps.detail: Assigns the Delta Regulation Group to the Delta Regulation Manager role.

steps.action: Click Apply.
steps.detail: Saves the role assignment and keeps the dialog open for additional assignments.

steps.action: Click the field above the Participant List and select Change Admin I.
steps.detail: Switches to assigning participants to the Change Admin I role.

steps.action: From the Search Results list, select Delta Change Group and click Add.
steps.detail: Assigns the Delta Change Group to the Change Admin I role.

steps.action: Click Apply.
steps.detail: Saves the Change Admin I role assignment.

steps.action: Click the field above the Participant List and select Change Admin II.
steps.detail: Switches to assigning participants to the Change Admin II role.

steps.action: From the Search Results list, select Delta Change Group and click Add.
steps.detail: Assigns the Delta Change Group to the Change Admin II role.

steps.action: Click Apply.
steps.detail: Saves the Change Admin II role assignment.

steps.action: Click the field above the Participant List and select Change Admin III.
steps.detail: Switches to assigning participants to the Change Admin III role.

steps.action: From the Search Results list, select Delta Change Group and click Add.
steps.detail: Assigns the Delta Change Group to the Change Admin III role.

steps.action: Click OK.
steps.detail: Saves all role assignments and closes the dialog. The Delta groups are now assigned to the appropriate roles on the Drill 1000 team.

keyTakeaways: Custom subtypes are created in the Type and Attribute Management utility and inherit all parent type properties.
User-defined groups are created on the organization Members page and can be assigned to team template roles.
The Delta Drills process requires two subtypes (Delta Review, Delta New Regulation Document) and two groups (Delta Regulation Group, Delta Change Group).
Assigning groups to team template roles enables automated task routing in change workflows.

topics.title: Configure Preferences and Templates

content.text: In this exercise, you set the Change Management preferences required for the Delta Drills process and create task form templates that automatically generate tasks when a Change Notice is submitted.

content.title: Configure Preferences and Templates
content.objective: Set change management preferences and create task form templates for the Delta Drills process.

steps.action: Open the Navigator and select Organizations > PTC Power Equipment > Utilities.
steps.detail: Navigates to the organization-level Utilities page.
steps.hint: You should be signed in to Windchill as mjones/ptc.

steps.action: In the Business Administration section, select Preference Management.
steps.detail: Preference Management is where you configure organization-level settings that control change management behavior.

steps.action: Expand the Change Management node and notice the available preferences.
steps.detail: The Change Management preference group contains all preferences that determine the behaviors of change objects.

steps.action:
Find each of the listed preferences and change the setting to the indicated value:
Change Management > Optional Assignee and Reviewer States: set to Open
Operation > CheckIn Operation > Auto Associate upon Check In: set to Yes
Workflow > Use task form template to generate the task details page: set to Yes
steps.detail: These three preferences enable key behaviors: assigning reviewers when editing Change Tasks, auto-associating objects on check-in, and using task form templates for task detail pages.
steps.hint: To change a preference, right-click the preference and select Set Preference from the context menu. In the Set Preference dialog, select the Locked checkbox and click OK for each preference you change.

steps.action: Verify each preference now shows the updated value and is locked.
steps.detail: Locking a preference ensures it will not be modified in any context located under the organization. A lock icon is displayed next to the preference name.
steps.hint: The Preference Management table is extensive. You can use the "Find in tree" field to find preferences by typing the first few letters or words of the preference followed by an asterisk (*).

steps.action: Open the Navigator and go to PTC Power Equipment > Templates.
steps.detail: Navigates to the organization-level Templates page where task form templates are managed.
steps.hint: You should be signed in to Windchill as mjones/ptc.

steps.action: From the Pick a View menu, select Task Form Templates.
steps.detail: Filters the Templates table to show only task form templates.

steps.action: Click the Create Task Form Template icon from the toolbar.
steps.detail: Opens the New Task Form Template dialog.

steps.action: Maximize the New Task Form Template dialog.
steps.detail: Maximizing the dialog makes it easier to see and configure all fields.

steps.action: In the Template Name field, type Create Delta Change Notice.
steps.detail: Names the template that will define the task when a Delta Change Notice is created.

steps.action: In the Subject Class field, select wt.change2.WTChangeReview|com.ptc_training.PTCPowerEquipment.DeltaReview.
steps.detail: Associates this task form template with the Delta Review object type you created earlier.

steps.action: If necessary, select Default from the Task Type list.
steps.detail: Default is the standard task type for change management workflows.

steps.action: Select the Configure in wizard radio button.
steps.detail: The wizard mode allows you to define tabs, actions, and tables for the task form.

steps.action: Click the Add New Tab icon in the Tabs field.
steps.detail: Creates a new tab that will organize the task form's content.

steps.action: Double-click New0 and select the text, type Details, and press Enter.
steps.detail: Renames the first tab to Details, which will contain the main task information.

steps.action: Click the Edit icon for Action on Subject.
steps.detail: Opens the list of available actions that can be performed from the task form.

steps.action: Select the checkboxes for: View Information, Edit, and New > New Change Notice.
steps.detail: These actions allow the task assignee to view, edit, and create a Change Notice from the task form.

steps.action: Click on white space in the dialog to close the list.
steps.detail: Closes the action selection dropdown.

steps.action: Click the Edit icon for Tables/Attributes.
steps.detail: Opens the list of tables and attributes that can be displayed on the tab.

steps.action: Select the checkboxes for: Task Completion Buttons, Attributes, Actions on Subject, Review Objects, and Associated Process Objects.
steps.detail: These tables and attributes will appear in the Details tab in the order the checkboxes are selected.
steps.hint: The tables and attributes listed above will appear in the tab in the order the checkboxes are selected.

steps.action: Click on white space in the dialog to close the list.
steps.detail: Closes the tables/attributes selection dropdown.

steps.action: Click the Add New Tab icon in the Tabs field.
steps.detail: Creates a second tab for organizing additional task information.

steps.action: Double-click New1 and select the text, type Actions/Processes, and press Enter.
steps.detail: Renames the second tab to Actions/Processes, which will contain workflow-related information.

steps.action: Click the Edit icon for Tables/Attributes.
steps.detail: Opens the list of tables and attributes for the second tab.

steps.action: Select the checkboxes for: Actions on Subject, Action Items, Process Status, Tasks for Change Process, and Task Completion.
steps.detail: These tables provide workflow context and completion controls on the second tab.

steps.action: Click on white space in the dialog to close the list.
steps.detail: Closes the tables/attributes selection dropdown.

steps.action: Click OK.
steps.detail: Creates the task form template with both tabs configured.

steps.action: Click the Import icon from the toolbar.
steps.detail: Opens the import dialog for loading pre-built task form templates.

steps.action: Click Choose File.
steps.detail: Opens the file browser to select the template file.

steps.action: Select This PC.
steps.detail: Navigates to the local file system.

steps.action: Select New Volume (W:)\WCBA-ADCH-Lab-Files\Task Flow Templates.zip.
steps.detail: This ZIP file contains the two pre-built task form templates for the Delta review and submit workflows.

steps.action: Click Open.
steps.detail: Loads the selected file into the import dialog.

steps.action: Click OK.
steps.detail: Imports the templates. Two new Task Form Templates are added to the Templates table: Delta Regulation – Review and Delta Regulation – Submit.

keyTakeaways: Change Management preferences are configured at the organization level under Preference Management.
The Task Form Templates preference must be enabled before templates can be created or used.
Task form templates automatically generate tasks with predefined names, assignees, and sequencing when a Change Notice is submitted.
The Delta Drills process requires three preferences enabled: CN without CR, Sequenced Plan, and Task Form Templates.

topics.title: Create Business Rules

content.text: In this exercise, you create the association and mapping rules needed for the Delta Drills regulation review process. These rules define how change objects link to each other and how change intents map to release targets.

You will use the Business Rules Administration page to create both the association rule and the mapping rule. Ensure the custom subtypes (Delta Review, Delta New Regulation Document) have been created before starting this exercise.

content.title: Create Business Rules
content.objective: Create association and mapping rules for the Delta Drills regulation review process.

steps.action: Open the Navigator and go to Organizations > PTC Power Equipment > Utilities.
steps.detail: Navigates to the organization-level Utilities page where business rules are managed.
steps.hint: You should be signed in to Windchill as mjones/ptc.

steps.action: Select Business Rules.
steps.detail: Opens the Business Rules Administration page with tables for Rule Sets, Association Rules, and Mapping Rules.

steps.action: From the Association Rules table, click New Association Rule.
steps.detail: Opens the New Association Rule dialog where you define the relationship between two change object types.

steps.action:
Fill in the fields of the New Association Rule dialog:
Association Type: Change Process
Role A Type: Delta Review
Role B Type: Change Notice
Cardinality: 1:Many
Owning Role: Role B
Required Role: None
steps.detail: Change Process links objects within the same change process flow. 1:Many cardinality means one Delta Review can link to many Change Notices. Setting Role B as the owning role means you need modify access to the Change Notice to create the link.

steps.action: Click OK.
steps.detail: Creates the association rule. This rule enables Delta Drills to associate multiple Change Notices with a single Delta Review.

tasks.title: Create a Change Intent Mapping Rule

steps.action: From the Mapping Rules table, click the New Mapping Rule icon.
steps.detail: Opens the New Mapping Rule dialog for defining how change intents map to release targets.
steps.hint: You should be signed in to Windchill as mjones/ptc.

steps.action:
Fill in the fields of the New Mapping Rule dialog:
Rule Name: Change Intent Release Targets
Type: Delta New Regulation Document
Source Value: None
Mapped Value: Change
Default: Change
steps.detail: This mapping rule sets the release target value for new release targets on Delta New Regulation Document objects. It maps the default change intent to 'Change', indicating the document triggers a product change.

steps.action: Click OK.
steps.detail: Creates the mapping rule. It is now active and will control how change intents are applied to Delta New Regulation Document objects in the Resulting Objects table.

keyTakeaways: Association rules and mapping rules are created in the Business Rules Administration utility.
The Delta Drills association rule uses Change Process type, linking Delta Review (Role A) to Change Notice (Role B) with 1:many cardinality.
Mapping rules define how change intents connect to release targets for specific object types.
Custom subtypes must exist before they can be referenced in association or mapping rules.
